thread.title: Texas State Board of Education OKs hotly debated state-created lessons with Bible stories
thread.title_full: Texas State Board of Education OKs hotly debated state-created lessons with Bible stories

author: Jay Janner
title: Texas State Board of Education OKs hotly debated state-created lessons with Bible stories
text: NEWS Jay JannerState Board of Education members, left to right, Julie Pickren, LJ Francis, Keven Ellis and Audrey Young discuss the controversial Bluebonnet teaching materials at a meeting of the State Board of Education at the William B. Travis Building Friday November 22, 2024. The Bluebonnet materials, which use Bible stories, are K-5 math and reading instructional materials that the 2023 House Bill 1605 mandated the Texas Education Agency created with the intention of creating high quality instructional resources for teachers.Jay Janner/American-StatesmanMembers of the State Board of Education vote on a motion during debate of the controversial Bluebonnet teaching materials at a meeting of the State Board of Education at the William B. Travis Building Friday November 22, 2024. The Bluebonnet materials, which use Bible stories, are K-5 math and reading instructional materials that the 2023 House Bill 1605 mandated the Texas Education Agency created with the intention of creating high quality instructional resources for teachers.Jay Janner/American-StatesmanState Board of Education member Will Hickman, middle, smiles after board approval of the controversial Bluebonnet teaching materials at a meeting of the State Board of Education at the William B. Travis Building Friday November 22, 2024. At left is board member Audrey Young. At right is board member Keven Ellis. The Bluebonnet materials, which use Bible stories, are K-5 math and reading instructional materials that the 2023 House Bill 1605 mandated the Texas Education Agency created with the intention of creating high quality instructional resources for teachers.Jay Janner/American-StatesmanState Board of Education member Staci Childs listens during discussion the controversial Bluebonnet teaching materials at a meeting of the State Board of Education at the William B.
